author: @e100vids2
text:
I read in the acetone article at wikipedia that it can be used as a type of polishing or smoothing for plastics:

Acetone is often used for vapor polishing of printing artifacts on 3D-printed models printed with ABS plastic. The technique, called acetone vapor bath smoothing, involves placing the printed part in a sealed chamber containing a small amount of acetone, and heating to around 80 degrees Celsius for 10 minutes. This creates a vapor of acetone in the container. The acetone condenses evenly all over the part, causing the surface to soften and liquefy. Surface tension then smooths the semi-liquid plastic. When the part is removed from the chamber, the acetone component evaporates leaving a glassy-smooth part free of striation, patterning, and visible layer edges, common features in untreated 3D printed parts.[75]

author: @totherarf
text:
I suspect it will excel at low wind speeds but possibly get overloaded at higher ones!
I wonder if variable pitch might sort that out a bit better though. Probably not worth the extra to make it though ........ unless you are going for mass production ;o)
Looking at real full sized turbines there seems to be other attributes that are not on their smaller cousins. One is Flexing tips to reduce high wind loads. Another is the curved but flat section of smaller blades compared to the more aerodynamic "plane wing" cross-section of larger blades!

Maybe these factors are because of the size differential though and are no more than academic with the sizes we are looking at here!
Eather way ..... More Brain Food. Thanks!

text: My guess (and it is no more than that), is that the other blades will work better at higher speed and provide more torque because of their design.  Definite potential though even if that is the case.  You can't change the blades in a proper wind turbine, but there is the potential to have something that changes their shape.  Think flaps on an aircraft.  We do sit in the wind doldrums for long periods in the UK and it would be good to get something out of wind turbines even when the wind is at a very low speed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
The fundamental aerodynamics of a horizontal axis wind turbine rotor and a propeller is identical. If you would run the wind turbine generator as a motor the turbine would generate thrust very efficiently. This is measured by the thrust coefficient. For a wind turbine at optimal conditions this is close to the maximum possible: 8/9 according to Betz' theory.

The reasons the shape is not the same are due to different scale in size and in air speed which leads to different structural considerations. The wind turbine blades must be thick because the rotational speed is quite slow. Therefore the beneficial effect of stress stiffening is not as pronounced as in a propeller blade. Also, the incoming wind is very turbulent compared to the wind seen by a propeller. Therefore it is beneficial with a thick blade that has a smooth transition to stall.

Ahh mate - that is wrong  The fundamental aerodynamics of a horizontal axis wind turbine rotor and a propeller is identical. If you would run the wind turbine generator as a motor the turbine would generate thrust very efficiently. This is measured by the thrust coefficient. For a wind turbine at optimal conditions this is close to the maximum possible: 8/9 according to Betz' theory.

The reasons the shape is not the same are due to different scale in size and in air speed which leads to different structural considerations. The wind turbine blades must be thick because the rotational speed is quite slow. Therefore the beneficial effect of stress stiffening is not as pronounced as in a propeller blade. Also, the incoming wind is very turbulent compared to the wind seen by a propeller. Therefore it is beneficial with a thick blade that has a smooth transition to stall.

author: @danedmiston9673
text:
To print a turbine version of the propeller create a mirror image of the blade, perpendicular to the center axis, and 3D print that file.
The mirror image can be done while building the STL file or with the slicing software during the placement operation for the printer .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text:
Thanks for sharing the blade files.
You're right!!!  The mirror image did not help.  The twist needs to be reversed to get the air to flow the right direction over the airfoils.  Those skinny curved airfoils seem to work just fine anyway.   @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @willcool713
replies.text: @christopherd.winnan8701 Oh, yeah, very true. But there's something about constricted options which can bring about increased creativity, too. Creative problem solving may be the lowest form of creativity, on its own, but it always adds to endeavors, sometimes synergistically. Too many options can overwhelm and confuse. Like Shakespeare's iambic pentameter, the limited palettes chosen by some of the Old Masters, science sticking entirely to reason alone, photographers working in black and white, specific rhyming schemes in Haiku, Limericks, and other poetry -- limitations sometimes produce better results more naturally. And I think it comes from being open to alternative solutions.

author: @tridsonline
text: 👍🏻 Interesting. But it reminds me of a related propeller design issue: what is the most silent design for a drone propeller? In particular, is the single-bladed propeller with a counter weight quieter than others? And if it is, then does it still provide enough thrust to be practical?

replies.author: @Rainyjax
replies.text:
Zipline drones seem to answer both of those questions. Considerably quieter than other drones of similar size, and still more than enough thrust to be competitive.
Those use two blades with a counterweight per propeller, though, instead of just one.
